annotations.label: System of linear equations

anchor.snippet: is a collection of two or more linear equations involving the same variables

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named 'system of linear equations' object; a system is encoded as the matrix-vector equation A *ᵥ x = b via Matrix.mulVec.

annotations.label: Three equations in three variables

anchor.snippet: is a system of three equations in the three variables

annotations.note: This is a specific illustrative example with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Solution to a linear system

anchor.snippet: A solution to a linear system is an assignment of values to the variables such that all the equations are simultaneously satisfied

annotations.note: A solution is simply a vector x with A *ᵥ x = b; Mathlib expresses this directly but has no standalone 'solution' definition.

annotations.label: One equation in one unknown

anchor.snippet: The system of one equation in one unknown

annotations.note: A trivial worked example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Two equations, two variables

anchor.snippet: The simplest kind of nontrivial linear system involves two equations and two variables

annotations.note: A worked numerical example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: General system form

anchor.snippet: A general system of m linear equations with n unknowns and coefficients can be written as

annotations.note: The general m×n system is captured by the matrix-vector product A *ᵥ x over arbitrary index types m, n.

annotations.label: Vector equation view

anchor.snippet: each unknown is a weight for a column vector in a linear combination

annotations.note: Matrix.mulVec_eq_sum states A *ᵥ v is the sum of the columns weighted by the entries of v, exactly the linear-combination-of-columns view.

annotations.label: Matrix equation form

anchor.snippet: The vector equation is equivalent to a matrix equation of the form

annotations.note: The matrix equation A x = b is exactly Matrix.mulVec (A *ᵥ x), with Matrix.mulVecLin giving its linear-map form.

anchors.snippet: The vector equation is equivalent to a matrix equation of the form

annotations.label: Solution set

anchor.snippet: The set of all possible solutions is called the solution set

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone 'solution set' notion, but for homogeneous systems it is LinearMap.ker of Matrix.mulVecLin and for general systems a coset thereof.

annotations.label: Three possible behaviors

anchor.snippet: A linear system may behave in any one of three possible ways

annotations.note: No single Mathlib theorem states a linear system has zero, one, or infinitely many solutions.

annotations.label: Two variables: lines

anchor.snippet: each linear equation determines a line on the xy - plane

annotations.note: The geometric interpretation of a two-variable equation as a line is not formalized as a statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three variables: planes

anchor.snippet: each linear equation determines a plane in three-dimensional space

annotations.note: The geometric interpretation of a three-variable equation as a plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: n variables: hyperplanes

anchor.snippet: each linear equation determines a hyperplane in n -dimensional space

annotations.note: The statement that a single linear equation cuts out a hyperplane is not formalized as such, though Mathlib has a separate notion of hyperplanes.

annotations.label: Underdetermined system

anchor.snippet: a system with fewer equations than unknowns has infinitely many solutions

annotations.note: This 'general behavior' heuristic (which can fail for inconsistent systems) has no matching Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Square system unique solution

anchor.snippet: a system with the same number of equations and unknowns has a single unique solution

annotations.note: The precise version—A square and a unit (full rank) gives a unique solution—is Matrix.mulVec_injective_iff_isUnit together with mulVec_surjective_iff_isUnit.

annotations.label: Overdetermined system

anchor.snippet: a system with more equations than unknowns has no solution

annotations.note: This 'general behavior' heuristic (which can fail for consistent overdetermined systems) has no matching Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Dimension of solution set

anchor.snippet: the dimension of the solution set is, in general, equal to n − m

annotations.note: The exact statement is rank–nullity (finrank_range_add_finrank_ker), giving dim of solution space = n − rank, which equals n − m only when the rows are independent.

annotations.label: Independent equations

anchor.snippet: The equations of a linear system are independent if none of the equations can be derived algebraically from the others

annotations.note: Independence of equations corresponds to linear independence of the coefficient rows, formalized via LinearIndependent and (for square A) linearIndependent_rows_iff_isUnit.

annotations.label: Scaled equation not independent

anchor.snippet: are not independent — they are the same equation when scaled by a factor of two

annotations.note: A specific example of dependent equations with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Sum of two equations not independent

anchor.snippet: are not independent, because the third equation is the sum of the other two

annotations.note: A specific example of dependent equations with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Consistent and inconsistent systems

anchor.snippet: A linear system is inconsistent if it has no solution, and otherwise, it is said to be consistent

annotations.note: Consistency (existence of a solution) is membership of b in the range of the linear map, i.e. LinearMap.mem_range, but 'consistent system' is not a named definition.

annotations.label: Inconsistent pair of equations

anchor.snippet: In fact, by subtracting the first equation from the second one

annotations.note: A specific worked example of an inconsistent pair with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Three pairwise-consistent inconsistent equations

anchor.snippet: It is possible for three linear equations to be inconsistent, even though any two of them are consistent together

annotations.note: A specific worked example with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Inconsistency and linear dependence

anchor.snippet: inconsistencies occur if the left-hand sides of the equations in a system are linearly dependent

annotations.note: Related infrastructure links dependence of rows/columns (det = 0) to a nontrivial kernel via exists_mulVec_eq_zero_iff, but the inconsistency-vs-dependence statement itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rouché–Capelli theorem

anchor.snippet: according to the Rouché–Capelli theorem , any system of equations (overdetermined or otherwise) is inconsistent if the rank of the augmented matrix is greater than the rank of the coefficient matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has Matrix.rank but no Rouché–Capelli theorem relating consistency to the rank of the augmented matrix.

annotations.label: Equivalent linear systems

anchor.snippet: Two linear systems using the same set of variables are equivalent if each of the equations in the second system can be derived algebraically

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of equivalence of linear systems.

annotations.label: Equivalent iff same solution set

anchor.snippet: It follows that two linear systems are equivalent if and only if they have the same solution set

annotations.note: Without a formalized notion of system equivalence, this characterization is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free variables

anchor.snippet: typically some of the variables are designated as free

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of free variables in a solved system.

annotations.label: Degrees of freedom

anchor.snippet: Each free variable gives the solution space one degree of freedom

annotations.note: The 'degrees of freedom' counting via free variables is not formalized, though it corresponds to the finrank of the kernel.

annotations.label: Elimination of variables method

anchor.snippet: The simplest method for solving a system of linear equations is to repeatedly eliminate variables

annotations.note: The variable-elimination solving algorithm is not formalized as a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Row reduction (Gaussian elimination)

anchor.snippet: In row reduction (also known as Gaussian elimination ), the linear system is represented as an augmented matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes reduction of a matrix to diagonal form by transvections, the algebraic core of Gaussian elimination, but not the augmented-matrix solving procedure.

annotations.label: Elementary row operations

anchor.snippet: There are three types of elementary row operations

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes transvections (the row-addition operation) via TransvectionStruct, but not a unified 'three elementary row operations' concept.

annotations.label: Row operations preserve equivalence

anchor.snippet: Because these operations are reversible, the augmented matrix produced always represents a linear system that is equivalent to the original

annotations.note: Reversibility of transvections is formalized (inv_mul gives an explicit inverse), but the resulting preservation of a system's solution set is not stated.

annotations.label: Cramer's rule

anchor.snippet: Cramer's rule is an explicit formula for the solution of a system of linear equations, with each variable given by a quotient of two determinants

annotations.note: Cramer's rule is Matrix.mulVec_cramer (A *ᵥ cramer A b = det A • b) together with Matrix.cramer_apply expressing each component as a determinant quotient.

annotations.label: Square full-rank matrix solution

anchor.snippet: If the matrix A is square (has m rows and n = m columns) and has full rank (all m rows are independent), then the system has a unique solution given by

annotations.note: For invertible (full-rank square) A, the unique solution x = A⁻¹ b is Matrix.inv_mulVec_eq_vec, with uniqueness from mulVec_injective_of_invertible.

annotations.label: Moore–Penrose general solution

anchor.snippet: all solutions (if any exist) are given using the Moore–Penrose inverse of A

annotations.note: Mathlib does not have the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse.

annotations.label: Existence condition for solutions

anchor.snippet: A necessary and sufficient condition for any solution(s) to exist is that the potential solution obtained using

annotations.note: The criterion A A⁺ b = b is stated via the Moore–Penrose inverse, which is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Iterative methods

anchor.snippet: This leads to the class of iterative methods

annotations.note: Iterative linear solvers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jacobi method

anchor.snippet: One example of an iterative method is the Jacobi method

annotations.note: The Jacobi iterative method is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Homogeneous system

anchor.snippet: A system of linear equations is homogeneous if all of the constant terms are zero

annotations.note: A homogeneous system A *ᵥ x = 0 is represented directly, its solution set being LinearMap.ker of Matrix.mulVecLin, but 'homogeneous system' is not a named definition.

annotations.label: Trivial solution exists

anchor.snippet: Every homogeneous system has at least one solution, known as the zero (or trivial ) solution

annotations.note: Matrix.mulVec_zero states A *ᵥ 0 = 0, i.e. the zero vector always solves a homogeneous system.

annotations.label: Closure of homogeneous solutions

anchor.snippet: If u and v are two vectors representing solutions to a homogeneous system, then the vector sum u + v is also a solution to the system

annotations.note: Matrix.mulVec_add (A *ᵥ (u+v) = A *ᵥ u + A *ᵥ v) immediately gives that the sum of two homogeneous solutions is a solution; the solution set is the submodule LinearMap.ker.

annotations.label: Solution set is null space

anchor.snippet: the solution set to a homogeneous system is the same as the null space of the corresponding matrix A

annotations.note: The null space is LinearMap.ker of Matrix.mulVecLin, whose membership (LinearMap.mem_ker with mulVecLin_apply) is exactly A *ᵥ v = 0.

annotations.label: Solution set as translation

anchor.snippet: if p is any specific solution to the linear system

annotations.note: LinearMap.sub_mem_ker_iff (x − p ∈ ker f ↔ f x = f p) shows every solution is a particular solution p plus a kernel element.

annotations.label: Geometric translation of solution sets

anchor.snippet: this says that the solution set for A x = b is a translation of the solution set for A x = 0

annotations.note: The algebraic content (solutions of Ax=b form a coset of ker) is sub_mem_ker_iff, but the geometric 'translation' interpretation is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Solution exists iff b in image

anchor.snippet: This occurs if and only if the vector b lies in the image of the linear transformation A

annotations.note: LinearMap.mem_range states b ∈ range f ↔ ∃ x, f x = b, exactly that a solution exists iff b is in the image (with Matrix.range_mulVecLin giving the span of columns).
